Q1.6.question_text: Based on Mr. G. Krishnan's insights from the international business workshop, discuss the Business Model Canvas for HTPL with reference to the information given in the case study and how the following elements of the model can streamline operations and uncover growth opportunities: 1. Key Partners, activities and resources 2. Customer relationships 3. Customer segments 4. Cost structure 5. Value proposition
Q1.6.model_answer:
Forward Hedge: Forward Rate ` 104/€, Amount required to be paid after 3-months (€ 15000 x 2) € 30,000. As of 1st November 2025, the spot exchange rate stands at ` 102 per Euro, with the three-month forward rate at ` 104 per Euro. Borrowing rates are 6% in Germany and 10% in India, while deposit rates are 4% and 7%, respectively. Additionally, the German branch of an Indian bank has offered credit at 6% per annum against the opening of an irrevocable letter of credit. Commission charges for issuance of letter of credit are at 2% per annum. The company currently utilizes cash credit and term loan facilities from its bank at an interest rate of 10% per annum. Interest is compounded monthly for borrowings and quarterly for deposits. Owing to a global rise in steel prices, XYZ GmbH increased the FOB price of the tea sachet forming machine by 3% over the previously agreed price. The Finance Manager received the email notification regarding the price revision on 25th January 2026 and the payment was made on 31st January 2026 in Euros, which were purchased in accordance with the hedging option selected by him. Exchange rate prescribed by RBI as on 31st January 2026 was ` 107 per Euro and exchange rate notified by CBIC was ` 106 per Euro. XYZ GmbH dispatched the machine by ship on 2nd February 2026. The ship carrying the machine arrived at Cochin Port on 6th March 2026 and the Bill of Entry was filed on 4th March 2026. Freight charges from Germany to India totalled Euro 5,000 and insurance charges of Euro 400 were paid on 31 January 2026. HTPL paid a commission of ` 1,61,300 to the local agent in India of the XYZ GmbH. Freight charges of ` 1,05,850 were paid to a local transport company for transporting the machine from Cochin Port to the factory situated at Kodagu, Karnataka. The machine was installed and put to use from 25th March 2026. The exchange rate notified by CBIC on the date of the Bill of Entry (04.03.2026) was ` 109 per Euro, while on the date of the ship's entry (06.03.2026) it was ` 108 per Euro. The Basic Customs Duty rate was 15% on the date of the Bill of Entry but was reduced to 10% with effect from 5th March 2026. The Social Welfare Surcharge is levied at 10% and the Integrated Tax (IGST) under the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 is 18%.

Based on Mr. G. Krishnan's insights from the international business workshop, discuss the Business Model Canvas for HTPL with reference to the information given in the case study and how the following elements of the model can streamline operations and uncover growth opportunities:
1. Key Partners, activities and resources
2. Customer relationships
3. Customer segments
4. Cost structure
5. Value proposition
(5 Marks)

Business Model Canvas given by Osterwalder:

Key Partners: Key Partnerships include domestic and international tea leaf suppliers to ensure flexible and reliable sourcing beyond owned estates; sustainable and recycled packaging vendors to support environmental commitments; external logistics providers to strengthen distribution capacity; research institutes and consultants to advance flavor innovation, soil health, and sustainable practices; and government that offer renewable energy incentives and sustainability certifications.

These collaborations enhance sourcing ability, reduce operational bottlenecks, strengthen sustainability performance, and improve brand credibility among eco-conscious consumers.

Key Activities: It includes tea cultivation (plucking, crop planning, workforce management, and leaf quality control) and processing (withering, rolling, fermentation, drying, grading. blending, and packaging). Operations are strengthened by efficient logistics and distribution through coordinated dispatches and optimized routing. Ongoing R&D ensures new flavor development, blend testing, and consistent quality, while sustainability efforts focus on waste conversion, solar energy, and eco-friendly packaging.

Standardized plucking cycles and efficient logistics reduce costs and improve productivity, and ongoing R&D strengthens product differentiation and competitive advantage.

Key Resources: It includes high-quality tea estates, efficient modern processing facilities, skilled human capital, sustainability infrastructure, and strong brand assets with proprietary blends and certifications.

Optimizing estate and factory operations reduces waste and rejection rates, while continued investment in R&D and sustainability strengthens brand equity and global competitiveness.

Customer Relationships: It focuses on delivering personalized service to customers responsive order support, while also engaging direct consumers via loyalty programs that encourage repeat purchases. The company leverages customer feedback through surveys and quality monitoring to continuously refine its blends.

Strong relationships with B2B and retail partners improve demand forecasting and inventory optimization, while feedback-driven R&D minimizes product rejection rates and enhances overall quality.

Customer Segments: HTPL serves diverse customers through internal channels, wholesalers, retailers, direct-to-consumer outlets, and international markets. Its clients include domestic consumers, retail and wholesale buyers, clients like hotels, export customers, and eco-conscious consumers. The company focuses on high value and international segments to boost margins and diversify revenue.

By segmenting its customers effectively, HTPL can tailor packaging sizes ranging from 100 g to 10 kg. implement differentiated pricing strategies and design targeted marketing campaigns. Furthermore, focusing on high-value and international segments provides opportunities to expand profit margins and diversify revenue streams, supporting long-term operational growth.

Channels: The company reaches customers through multiple channels, including company-owned retail stores, wholesalers and distributors across urban and rural areas and partnerships with overseas retailers for exports.

Operationally, clustering distribution routes and sharing logistics help reduce costs, while brand awareness enhances margins.

Cost Structure: This includes variable costs such as labor for plucking and factory operations, raw materials, packaging, logistics, and utilities, alongside fixed costs like estate maintenance, factory depreciation, R&D, marketing, and solar infrastructure. Sustainability investments cover solar panels, waste management systems, recycled packaging, and energy efficiency initiatives.

Operational efficiencies through estate benchmarking and optimized logistics reduce idle hours and fuel expenses, enabling competitive pricing against new market entrants without compromising quality.

Revenue Stream: The business generates revenue through multiple streams, including retail sales of packaged tea (wholesale and B2B supply to hotels, restaurants, and shops, as well as exports to international and specialty markets. Additional income comes from value-added offerings such as premium blends, organic teas, and eco-friendly packaging.

This diversified approach reduces reliance on any single market while premium and export products provide higher-margin growth opportunities.

Value Proposition: HTPL stands out by offering high-quality tea, carefully sourced and processed for consistent purity, aroma, and flavour. Its integrated operations from plantation to packaging ensure strict quality control, while sustainability initiatives like solar-powered factories, recycled packaging, and waste-to-compost programs appeal to eco-conscious consumers. The company also emphasizes product variety and innovation through unique blends developed via R&D, supported by reliable supply through efficient logistics and standardized plucking cycles.

These strengths in quality, sustainability, and innovation enable differentiation in a crowded market and create opportunities for premium pricing.

ALTERNATIVE

The Business Model Canvas (BMC) helps a company understand how different parts of the business work together to create value, improve efficiency and identify growth opportunities. Based on the insights gained

by Mr. G. Krishnan during the international workshop, HTPL can use the Business Model Canvas to strengthen its operations, improve customer satisfaction and support long-term sustainable growth.
1.  Key Partners, Key Activities and Key Resources

Key Partners: HTPL's important partners include tea leaf suppliers from Sri Lanka, wholesalers, retailers, supermarkets, hotels, restaurants, logistics providers and sustainable packaging suppliers. The company also works with renewable energy solution providers for solar power generation. These partnerships help the company maintain quality standards, ensure smooth supply chain operations and strengthen market reach.

The decision to manufacture tea sachets in-house instead of depending completely on external suppliers would further improve control over quality, size and design of sachets. This can reduce dependency on outside vendors and improve operational efficiency.

Key Activities: The key activities of HTPL include tea cultivation, manual tea plucking, tea processing, blending, quality testing, packaging, distribution, marketing and customer feedback analysis. Sustainability activities such as solar power generation, use of recycled packaging and converting tea waste into fertilisers are also important operational activities.

The company has already streamlined operations by standardizing plucking cycles and improving logistics through clustered routing and shared vehicle usage. These measures have reduced idle time, improved leaf quality and lowered operational costs.

Key Resources: HTPL's major resources include its tea estates, manufacturing plants, skilled workforce, research and development team, strong brand reputation and global distribution network. The company's renewable energy infrastructure is also valuable resource.

Its strong research and development capabilities help in introducing innovative tea flavours and premium products such as organic and green tea, which support future growth opportunities.
2.  Customer Relationships: HTPL focuses strongly on maintaining long-term relationships with customers through dedicated order support and regular customer feedback systems. The company continuously monitors customer preferences and uses surveys and quality checks to improve its tea blends and services.

By offering consistent product quality, personalized support and innovative tea products, HTPL builds customer trust and loyalty. Expansion into premium tea, organic tea and health-focused products also helps the company meet changing consumer preferences and strengthen customer relationships.

The company's sustainability initiatives and CSR activities further improve brand image and customer goodwill, especially among environmentally conscious consumers.
3.  Customer Segments: HTPL serves multiple customer segments in both domestic and international markets. These include:
• Retail customers purchasing packaged tea products
• Supermarkets and retail chains
• Hotels and restaurants
• International buyers across more than 50 countries
• Health-conscious consumers preferring organic and green tea
• Bulk buyers purchasing large packaging sizes

By offering in different pack sizes ranging from 100 grams to 10 kilograms, HTPL is able to cater to different customer needs and preferences.

The growing demand for premium blends, herbal tea and health-oriented tea products provides additional growth opportunities for the company in both Indian and international markets.
4.  Cost Structure: HTPL's major costs include plantation expenses, labour costs for manual plucking, manufacturing costs, packaging expenses, transportation and logistics costs, research and development expenses and marketing costs.

Earlier, the company faced higher operational costs due to longer plucking cycles and inefficient transportation systems. However, standardization of plucking schedules and logistics optimization have helped reduce idle time, improve productivity and lower fuel costs.

The installation of solar panels has reduced electricity expenses and dependence on conventional energy sources. Similarly, use of recycled packaging materials and conversion of tea waste into fertilisers reduce waste management costs and support cost efficiency.

The decision to manufacture sachets internally may initially require investment in machinery and setup costs, but in the long run it can reduce procurement costs and improve quality control.
5.  Value Proposition: HTPL's main value proposition lies in providing high-quality tea products with consistent taste, purity and aroma. The company differentiates itself through premium quality tea leaves, modern processing facilities, innovative tea blends and sustainable business practices.

Its strong focus on quality control at every stage from tea plucking to blending and packaging helps maintain customer trust and brand reputation. The company also creates value through personalized customer service, environmentally responsible operations and continuous product innovation.

The introduction of premium blends, organic tea and green tea allows HTPL to meet changing customer preferences and growing health consciousness. Sustainability initiatives such as renewable energy usage, recycled packaging and waste recycling further strengthen the company's value proposition and enhance its image as an environmentally responsible brand.

Overall, by developing a clear Business Model Canvas, HTPL can better understand its strengths, improve operational coordination, reduce inefficiencies and identify future growth opportunities in both domestic and global tea markets.

Q1.8.question_text: Computation of depreciation on plant and machinery (Block 15%) for the A.Y. 2026-27. WDV as on 1.4.2025 is ` 120 crore. New machinery and equipment acquired on 10.8.2025 including freight and installation charges ` 2.55 crore. Imported tea sachet forming machinery put to use 25.3.2026. Insurance receipts for loss of machinery in fire ` 1.40 crore. Compute the total depreciation for P.Y. 2025-26 including additional depreciation, and compute the actual cost of imported tea sachet forming machinery.
Q1.8.model_answer:
Computation of depreciation on plant and machinery (Block 15%) for the A.Y. 2026-27. WDV as on 1.4.2025 is ` 120 crore. New machinery and equipment acquired on 10.8.2025 including freight and installation charges: ` 2.50 crore (machinery) + ` 0.05 crore (freight and installation) = ` 2.55 crore, installed and put to use on 15th November 2025. ` 0.50 crore spent on office furnishing and renovation (including computers for ` 0.25 crore installed in office). ` 1.90 crore on installation of solar panels for use in the factory and plantation estates. HTPL is maintaining a Tea Development Account with NABARD as per the scheme approved by Tea Board. The Written Down Value (WDV) of the machinery and equipment block (Rate of Depreciation: 15%) as per the Income-tax Act, 1961 is ` 120 crore as at 1st April 2025. Company placed an order for new machinery and equipment (15% Block) costing ` 2.50 crore on 10th August 2025 by withdrawing ` 5 crore from the Tea Development Account. HTPL sold a parcel of land in November 2025 for ` 3 crore. The land had originally been acquired in January 2023 for ` 1.50 crore using funds withdrawn from the Tea Development Account, which were intended for the upgradation of the existing infrastructure of the company's unit. However, the company utilized these funds to purchase the land with the objective of constructing a warehouse. The proposed warehouse could not be developed due to the failure to obtain the necessary approvals from the local government authorities. An order was placed on 1st November 2025 for two customised tea sachet forming machines with CNC system and PLC controls (15% Block) from XYZ GmbH situated in Germany at a FOB price of Euro 15,000 each to be despatched in first week of February 2026. The machine was installed and put to use from 25th March 2026.

Q2.6.question_text: Roshan Joshi believes that the operational inefficiencies in Heritage Organic are a necessary part of the artisanal process. However, Geeta Sharma plans to eliminate them. Based on the information in the given scenario, explain which of the identified 'Non-Value-Added' activities can be eliminated and what will be its strategic implication?
Q2.6.model_answer:
Several activities in Heritage Organic's operations can be classified as 'Non-Value-Added (NVA) activities. An NVA activity is defined as work that is not valued by the customer, does not improve the quality or function of a product or service, creates waste, and adds costs for which the customer is not willing to pay. 

The identified NVA activities in Heritage Organic are: 

Moving: The manual process of moving partially aged products from the fermentation facility to the aging facility is a "Moving" activity. The scenario defines this as an activity that uses time and resources to move materials and work-in-process from one department to another. Customers do not value this movement; they only care about the final quality and timely delivery of the product. 

Waiting: The delays of finished bottled spirits in the temporary holding bay waiting for quality control checks is a "Waiting" activity. The scenario defines this as an activity where materials or work-in-process use time and resources by waiting for the next process. This delay adds no value to the final product. 

Inspecting: The comprehensive inspection of every tenth bottle is an "Inspecting" activity. The scenario states that inspecting is a wasteful activity if the product was produced correctly the first time. While it ensures quality, the ideal state is to have a process so robust that inspection becomes unnecessary. 

Storing: The long periods that finished goods spend in the storage facility before dispatch is a "Storing" activity. The scenario defines this as using time and resources while a good or material is held in inventory. This is generally considered wasteful unless, like in the example of the wine manufacturer, the storage time itself adds to the product's quality. Since the balsamic vinegar and spirits are aged in a separate process, and the storage of finished goods is causing delays, it is a non-value-added activity.

Scheduling: The haphazard scheduling of production runs is a "Scheduling" activity. The scenario states that this is not necessary if the company knows how to produce on demand. This inefficiency leads to poor resource utilization and increased costs.

The strategic implication of eliminating these NVA activities is to reduce waste, optimize resource utilization, and lower operational costs. By focusing on cross-functional processes and identifying these wasteful activities, Geeta can enhance the company's performance, which in turn will positively impact the company's valuation through increased future cash flows.
ALTERNATIVE

The internal audit report of Heritage Organic Limited highlights several activities that do not add value to the product and can therefore be classified as non-value-added activities. These activities consume time, labour and resources but do not improve the taste, quality or uniqueness of the company's products. Customers are willing to pay for the traditional ageing process and artisanal quality of the balsamic vinegar, but they are not willing to pay for delays, unnecessary movement of goods, idle time or inefficient processes. Hence, such activities should be reduced or eliminated without affecting the traditional identity of the company.

One major non-value-added activity is the labour-intensive movement of partially aged products between facilities. The transportation of products from one location to another does not improve the quality of the vinegar and only increases handling time and logistics cost.

Way Out -> The company can reduce this inefficiency through better layout planning, automated material handling systems and improved coordination between facilities. This would reduce transportation cost, save time and improve operational efficiency.

Another non-value-added activity identified in the process is the unnecessary waiting time arising when barrels remain unattended in the staging area due to forklift operators being engaged in other non-essential activities. This idle time does not enhance the quality or value of the product; instead, it disrupts the smooth flow of operations, increases handling delays and reduces overall operational efficiency.

Way Out -> The company can address this inefficiency through proper scheduling of material handling activities, deployment of dedicated forklift operators and better coordination between production and warehouse teams. Use of real-time tracking and workflow planning systems can further streamline movement of barrels across facilities. Eliminating such delays would ensure uninterrupted workflow, faster processing and better utilisation of manpower and equipment.

The delay in quality control checks is also a major bottleneck. Finished bottled spirits remain in temporary holding areas waiting for inspection due to shortage of inspectors. Although quality inspection is important, excessive waiting does not add value for customers.

Way Out -> The company can improve this process by increasing inspection capacity, using automated quality testing methods and introducing quality checks during the production process itself. This would reduce delays and improve delivery speed.

Similarly, the inspection of 10% of total production consumes significant time and human resources. Excessive inspection is considered a non-value-added activity because quality should ideally be built into the production process rather than checked after production.

Way Out -> The company can adopt practices such as Total Quality Management (TQM), employee self-check systems and process control techniques to reduce dependence on large-scale inspections. This would lower inspection cost and improve process reliability.

The storage facility of the company is also highly disorganised, resulting in finished goods remaining in warehouses for long periods before dispatch. Excess inventory and prolonged storage do not create any value for customers and only increase storage and carrying costs.

Way Out -> Better inventory management, warehouse organisation and demand forecasting can help reduce unnecessary storage time. This would improve inventory turnover and cash flow position.

Further, production delays caused by unscheduled machine maintenance are also non-value-added activities. Machine breakdowns interrupt production and create delays without contributing to product quality.

Way Out -> The company can introduce preventive and predictive maintenance systems to reduce unexpected breakdowns. This would improve production continuity and machine utilisation. The company can also adopt Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) practices, where both maintenance staff and machine operators collectively work towards improving equipment effectiveness and reducing breakdowns. Further, the company may use Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) as a performance measurement tool to monitor machine availability, operational efficiency and quality output.

Strategic implication

The strategic implication of eliminating these non-value-added activities is highly significant for Heritage Organic Limited. The company can reduce operational costs, improve efficiency and respond faster to market demand while still preserving its traditional artisanal methods. The traditional fermentation and ageing process, which forms the core identity of the brand, can continue unchanged. However, operational inefficiencies such as waiting time, unnecessary movement, excessive storage and production delays should be removed because they do not contribute to customer value.

Therefore, the company should focus on maintaining the traditional craftsmanship that differentiates its products in the market while modernising inefficient operational processes. This balanced approach would help Heritage Organic improve profitability, protect market share and achieve long-term sustainable growth without losing its heritage value.

Consequences of buy back of shares: (i) Consequences of buy back of shares in the hands of shareholder and its obligation - In case of buyback of shares by Heritage Organic, the sum of ` 150 per share...

Q2.8.question_text: CA Gaurav should give following advice to the company regarding: Tax liability - An activity or transaction is considered as supply only when it is supply of goods or services or both for consideration in course or furtherance of business. However, Schedule I of the CGST Act, 2017 specifies exceptions where an activity or transaction is deemed to be a supply even without consideration. One such exception is gifts exceeding ` 50,000 in value in a financial year by an employer to an employee. Since in the given case, amount of gift does not exceed ` 50,000 per employee, gifting of home appliances to employees and workers does not amount to supply. ITC admissibility - Following ITC availed is inadmissible: (i) ITC of ` 150 Lakh is not admissible in the month of March 2024, since goods have not been received in said month. (ii) ITC of ` 2.70 lakh is not admissible in November 2023 since ITC on outdoor catering is blocked if the same is not used for making an outward supply of outdoor catering or as an element of a taxable composite/mixed supply. (iii) ITC on gifts to employees is inadmissible since ITC on goods disposed of by way of gift is blocked under section 17(5). Therefore, above credit will be reversed. Interest and penalty - Interest under section 50 is payable on amount of utilized ITC from the date of utilisation of such wrongly availed ITC (i.e. 18th April, 2024, earlier of GSTR-3B due date or filing date) till the date of reversal of such credit or payment of tax in respect of such amount @ 15% per annum. The wrongly availed ITC is deemed to be utilised to the extent the Electronic Credit Ledger balance falls below such ITC. Thus, interest is payable on ITC utilised, i.e. ` 42.60 lakh [(150 Lakh + ` 2.70 lakh + 9.90 lakh) - ` 120 lakh]. Penalty as per the provisions of section 74A, will be applicable.
Q2.8.model_answer:
Several activities in Heritage Organic's operations can be classified as 'Non-Value-Added (NVA) activities. An NVA activity is defined as work that is not valued by the customer, does not improve the quality or function of a product or service, creates waste, and adds costs for which the customer is not willing to pay. 

The identified NVA activities in Heritage Organic are: 

Moving: The manual process of moving partially aged products from the fermentation facility to the aging facility is a "Moving" activity. The scenario defines this as an activity that uses time and resources to move materials and work-in-process from one department to another. Customers do not value this movement; they only care about the final quality and timely delivery of the product. 

Waiting: The delays of finished bottled spirits in the temporary holding bay waiting for quality control checks is a "Waiting" activity. The scenario defines this as an activity where materials or work-in-process use time and resources by waiting for the next process. This delay adds no value to the final product. 

Inspecting: The comprehensive inspection of every tenth bottle is an "Inspecting" activity. The scenario states that inspecting is a wasteful activity if the product was produced correctly the first time. While it ensures quality, the ideal state is to have a process so robust that inspection becomes unnecessary. 

Storing: The long periods that finished goods spend in the storage facility before dispatch is a "Storing" activity. The scenario defines this as using time and resources while a good or material is held in inventory. This is generally considered wasteful unless, like in the example of the wine manufacturer, the storage time itself adds to the product's quality. Since the balsamic vinegar and spirits are aged in a separate process, and the storage of finished goods is causing delays, it is a non-value-added activity.

Scheduling: The haphazard scheduling of production runs is a "Scheduling" activity. The scenario states that this is not necessary if the company knows how to produce on demand. This inefficiency leads to poor resource utilization and increased costs.

The strategic implication of eliminating these NVA activities is to reduce waste, optimize resource utilization, and lower operational costs. By focusing on cross-functional processes and identifying these wasteful activities, Geeta can enhance the company's performance, which in turn will positively impact the company's valuation through increased future cash flows.
ALTERNATIVE

The internal audit report of Heritage Organic Limited highlights several activities that do not add value to the product and can therefore be classified as non-value-added activities. These activities consume time, labour and resources but do not improve the taste, quality or uniqueness of the company's products. Customers are willing to pay for the traditional ageing process and artisanal quality of the balsamic vinegar, but they are not willing to pay for delays, unnecessary movement of goods, idle time or inefficient processes. Hence, such activities should be reduced or eliminated without affecting the traditional identity of the company.

One major non-value-added activity is the labour-intensive movement of partially aged products between facilities. The transportation of products from one location to another does not improve the quality of the vinegar and only increases handling time and logistics cost.

Way Out -> The company can reduce this inefficiency through better layout planning, automated material handling systems and improved coordination between facilities. This would reduce transportation cost, save time and improve operational efficiency.

Another non-value-added activity identified in the process is the unnecessary waiting time arising when barrels remain unattended in the staging area due to forklift operators being engaged in other non-essential activities. This idle time does not enhance the quality or value of the product; instead, it disrupts the smooth flow of operations, increases handling delays and reduces overall operational efficiency.

Way Out -> The company can address this inefficiency through proper scheduling of material handling activities, deployment of dedicated forklift operators and better coordination between production and warehouse teams. Use of real-time tracking and workflow planning systems can further streamline movement of barrels across facilities. Eliminating such delays would ensure uninterrupted workflow, faster processing and better utilisation of manpower and equipment.

The delay in quality control checks is also a major bottleneck. Finished bottled spirits remain in temporary holding areas waiting for inspection due to shortage of inspectors. Although quality inspection is important, excessive waiting does not add value for customers.

Way Out -> The company can improve this process by increasing inspection capacity, using automated quality testing methods and introducing quality checks during the production process itself. This would reduce delays and improve delivery speed.

Similarly, the inspection of 10% of total production consumes significant time and human resources. Excessive inspection is considered a non-value-added activity because quality should ideally be built into the production process rather than checked after production.

Way Out -> The company can adopt practices such as Total Quality Management (TQM), employee self-check systems and process control techniques to reduce dependence on large-scale inspections. This would lower inspection cost and improve process reliability.

The storage facility of the company is also highly disorganised, resulting in finished goods remaining in warehouses for long periods before dispatch. Excess inventory and prolonged storage do not create any value for customers and only increase storage and carrying costs.

Way Out -> Better inventory management, warehouse organisation and demand forecasting can help reduce unnecessary storage time. This would improve inventory turnover and cash flow position.

Further, production delays caused by unscheduled machine maintenance are also non-value-added activities. Machine breakdowns interrupt production and create delays without contributing to product quality.

Way Out -> The company can introduce preventive and predictive maintenance systems to reduce unexpected breakdowns. This would improve production continuity and machine utilisation. The company can also adopt Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) practices, where both maintenance staff and machine operators collectively work towards improving equipment effectiveness and reducing breakdowns. Further, the company may use Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) as a performance measurement tool to monitor machine availability, operational efficiency and quality output.

Strategic implication

The strategic implication of eliminating these non-value-added activities is highly significant for Heritage Organic Limited. The company can reduce operational costs, improve efficiency and respond faster to market demand while still preserving its traditional artisanal methods. The traditional fermentation and ageing process, which forms the core identity of the brand, can continue unchanged. However, operational inefficiencies such as waiting time, unnecessary movement, excessive storage and production delays should be removed because they do not contribute to customer value.

Therefore, the company should focus on maintaining the traditional craftsmanship that differentiates its products in the market while modernising inefficient operational processes. This balanced approach would help Heritage Organic improve profitability, protect market share and achieve long-term sustainable growth without losing its heritage value.

feedback.what_went_right: You correctly identified the core concept of deferred tax arising on acquisition and understood that temporary differences between fair values and tax bases drive the DTL calculation. You also correctly included the Retirement Benefit Obligation in your net assets table and arrived at the right net DTL figure of ₹45 lakhs. (i) In respect of appeal preferred by HCL before the CIT (A) for A.Y. 2023-24

HCL has filed an appeal against the order of the Assessing Officer against certain additions. While appeal was pending disposal, the CIT invoked its revisionary power u/s 263 in respect of other issues which have not been dealt with in the assessment order.

The doctrine of partial merger holds good for section 263. Where the order passed by the A.O. has been the subject matter of any appeal, the powers of the Commissioner u/s 263 shall extend to such matters as had not been considered and decided in such appeal.

In this case, the commissioner can invoke its revisionary power u/s 263 in respect of other issues which have not been dealt with in the assessment order. Even for the subject matter of appeal, it has not been yet considered and decided in the CIT(A).

Accordingly, the view of tax counsel that the doctrine of total merger would apply in this case and the revision proposed is bad in law is not correct.

(ii) In respect of appeal preferred by HCL before the Appellate Tribunal for A.Y. 2024-25

HCL has filed an appeal against the order of the CIT(A) against the denial of depreciation. For disallowance u/s 40A(2), it did not file the appeal but filed a revision petition before commissioner u/s 264.

The concept of total merger would apply in case of section 264. The Commissioner of Income-tax has no power to revise any order under section 264, if the order has been made subject to an appeal to the Appellate Tribunal, even if the relief claimed in the revision is different from the relief claimed in the appeal and irrespective of the fact whether the appeal is by the assessee or by the Department.

Accordingly, the view of tax counsel that the action of the commissioner is not tenable in law is not correct.

Q4.7.question_text: Discuss the considerations when accepting an engagement to audit a single financial statement or a specific element of a financial statement, and the considerations when planning and performing such an audit, with reference to SA 805, SA 800, SA 200 and other relevant SAs. Also discuss how R & Co. LLP should approach the engagement as statutory auditor of VHIL's complete financial statements while auditing a specific element.
Q4.7.model_answer:
Per Ind AS 2 'Inventories', most by-products as well as scrap or waste materials, by their nature, are immaterial. They are often measured at net realisable value and this value is deducted from the cost of the main product.

4.7 Considerations When Accepting Such Engagement: As per SA 805, a 
single financial statement or a specific element of a financial statement may 
be prepared in accordance with an applicable financial reporting 
framework that is based on a financial reporting framework established by 
an authorised or recognised standards setting organisation for the 
preparation of a complete set of financial statements [e.g., Financial 
Reporting Framework]. If this is the case, determination of the acceptability 
of the applicable framework may involve considering whether that 
framework includes all the requirements of the framework on which it is 
based that are relevant to the presentation of a single financial statement 
or of a specific element of a financial statement that provides adequate 
disclosures.  
 
SA 800 states that in case of special purpose financial statements, the 
financial information needs of the intended users are a key factor in 
determining the acceptability of the financial reporting framework applied 
in the preparation of the financial statements. The applicable financial 
reporting framework may encompass the financial reporting standards 
established by an organisation that is authorised or recognised to 
promulgate standards for special purpose financial statements.  
 
The applicable financial reporting framework may encompass the financial 
reporting provisions of a contract or sources other than those described in 
the preceding paras. In that case, the acceptability of the financial reporting 
framework in the circumstances of the engagement is determined by 
considering whether the framework exhibits attributes normally exhibited 
by acceptable financial reporting frameworks in accordance with the 
requirements of SA 210.   
 
Considerations When Planning and Performing Such Audit: SA 200 
requires the auditor to comply with (a) relevant ethical requirements, 
including those pertaining to independence, relating to financial statement 
audit engagements, and (b) all SAs relevant to the audit.  
 
It also requires the auditor to comply with each requirement of an SA 
unless, in the circumstances of the audit, the entire SA is not relevant, or 
the requirement is not relevant because it is conditional and the condition 
does not exist. In exceptional circumstances, the auditor may judge it 
necessary to depart from a relevant requirement in an SA by performing 
alternative audit procedures to achieve the aim of that requirement.  
 
Application of some of the requirements of the SAs in an audit of special 
purpose financial statements may require special consideration by the 
auditor.  
 
In planning and performing the audit of a single financial statement or of 
a specific element of a financial statement, the auditor shall adapt all SAs 
relevant to the audit as necessary in the circumstances of the engagement. 
The relevance of each of the SAs requires careful consideration. Even when 
only a specific element of a financial statement is the subject of the audit, 
SAs such as SA 240, SA 550 and SA 570(Revised) are, in principle, 
relevant. This is because the element could be misstated as a result of 
fraud, the effect of related party transactions, or the incorrect 
application of the going concern basis of accounting under the 
applicable financial reporting framework.  
 
SA 260(Revised) requires the auditor to determine the appropriate 
person(s) within the entity's governance structure with whom to 
communicate.  
 
When auditing a single financial statement or a specific element of a 
financial statement in conjunction with the audit of the entity's complete 
set of financial statements, the auditor may be able to use audit evidence 
obtained as part of the audit of the entity's complete set of financial 
statements in the audit of the financial statement or the element. SAs,

however, require the auditor to plan and perform the audit of the financial statement or element to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence on which to base the opinion on the financial statement or on the element. 
 
The individual financial statements that comprise a complete set of financial statements, and many of the elements of those financial statements, including their related notes, are interrelated. Accordingly, when auditing a single financial statement or a specific element of a financial statement, the auditor may not be able to consider the financial statement or the element in isolation. Consequently, the auditor may need to perform procedures in relation to the interrelated items to meet the objective of the audit.  
 
Therefore, while accepting and performing the engagement, R & Co. LLP should comply with SA 800 (Revised) and SA 805 (Revised) and adapt all relevant SAs to the circumstances of the engagement. Although the firm is already statutory auditor of VHIL's complete financial statements and may use evidence obtained therein, it must independently plan and perform procedures to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence regarding the specific element. The auditor should also consider governance communication requirements, interrelationship of financial statement items, applicability of fraud/related party/going concern considerations, and separate materiality determination for the specific element under audit.

Pricing strategy for automated tunnelling machinery in the African Market: 
1.  It is stated by two market research firms that this product is a unique product and has no such competitor in the African market. This would mean that VHIL has abundant scope for making the product prominent besides earning huge profit margin. If the market report is correct, the product is first of its nature, the 'skimming pricing' would be the most appropriate pricing strategy. 
2.  If the product has to gain foothold in the market by making the public or the users to be aware of its availability, it is an evolutionary product and by means of mega advertisement demand can be generated and market hold could be achieved. The price can be determined by taking cost-benefit, competitor and demand for the product into account. 
3.  If the market already has players or potential entry of other machinery manufacturer, the company has to adopt either a cost based pricing or mark up pricing to play cautiously in the market. 
4.  If the market information is that the tunnelling machinery is a Me-too product, the company has to resort to penetration pricing to gain foothold in the market as in this case market price is determined by competitive forces for a successful product. 
5.  All the above strategies could be pursued either by incorporating a subsidiary company or by means of joint venture with a local player subject to due diligence. 
ALTERNATIVE 
 
VHIL should adopt a flexible and carefully planned pricing strategy while entering the African market because the market research reports provide mixed opinions.  
 
Some reports suggest that there is no similar high-end tunnelling machine available in the market, while other reports indicate that customers are conservative and strong competitors already exist. Therefore, the company should not depend on only one pricing method and should design its strategy according to market conditions and customer acceptance. 
 
Since automated tunnelling machinery is a highly advanced and expensive product used in large infrastructure projects, customers may hesitate to make huge upfront investments. Therefore, instead of only selling the machinery, VHIL can also offer the machines on lease through a subsidiary company. Leasing would reduce the financial burden on customers and help the company generate regular long-term income. It would also help the company attract customers who may not be willing to purchase the machinery immediately. 
 
In the initial stage, VHIL may adopt a penetration strategy by offering the machinery at comparatively attractive prices or with flexible payment terms to gain entry into the market and build customer confidence. This would help the company establish its presence and increase acceptance of the product. However, the company should avoid very low pricing because the machinery represents advanced technology and premium quality. Extremely low pricing may negatively affect the premium image of the product. 
 
Once the product gains market acceptance, VHIL can gradually shift towards value-based pricing. Under this approach, the company would charge prices based on the benefits provided by the machinery such as faster project completion, better efficiency, lower labour requirement and advanced technology. Infrastructure companies usually focus more on long-term cost savings and project efficiency rather than only the initial purchase price. 
 
If the product is viewed as a "Me-too" product because of existing competitors, VHIL may adopt competitive pricing by keeping prices comparable with market players while differentiating itself through better after-sales service, maintenance support, spare parts availability and operator training. Such additional support services can improve customer satisfaction and create competitive advantage. 
 
The company should also follow geographical pricing depending upon local conditions in different African countries. Pricing may vary based on transportation cost, import duties, economic conditions and infrastructure development of each region. 
 
Entering into a joint venture with a local player can further strengthen VHIL's pricing strategy. A local partner would have better knowledge of customer preferences, government procedures and local business practices. This would help VHIL reduce market entry risk and operational costs. Due to lower setup and marketing costs, the company may be able to offer more competitive pricing. A local partner would also improve customer trust and acceptance, especially in conservative markets. 
 
The joint venture can also help VHIL share costs relating to marketing, servicing and distribution. This would reduce the financial burden on the company and provide greater pricing flexibility. Further, local partners may help in obtaining government projects and local business opportunities more easily. 
 
Considering the contradictory market reports, VHIL should initially enter the market gradually through pilot projects, selective leasing arrangements and strategic partnerships instead of making large aggressive investments

Q_CaseStudy_Part15.question_text: Case Scenario: Avigo Airlines operational crisis, financial performance, bond refinancing, lease arrangement, and TDS survey issues. (i) Company has made payment of lease rent of ₹24 crore to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Limited located in Gujarat International Finance Tec-City for lease of an aircraft which commenced in June 2025. Neither tax has been deducted on lease rent paid during the year nor these payments have been reported in TDS returns filed by the company. (ii) Company has paid landing and parking charges of ₹12 crore to Airport Authority of India and ₹8 crore to private airport operators. The company has deducted tax @ 2% instead of 10% as the payments are in the nature of rent paid for use of space. (iii) Company has taken an engine on lease in financial year 2025-26 under an arrangement with a foreign company, a tax resident of Germany. Company deducted tax at source @ 10% on lease rental paid as per the provisions contained under DTAA between India and Germany. Chief Accountant informed that foreign company has no permanent establishment in India. However, revenue officers contended that in the absence of furnishing of PAN, TDS is to be deducted at a higher rate of 20% as per the relevant provisions of Income-tax Act, 1961. (iv) The Company allowed exemptions and deductions to its employees during financial year 2024-25 relevant to Assessment Year 2025-26 on account of various items.
Q_CaseStudy_Part15.model_answer:
In March 2026, anticipating a decline in market interest rates, the Chief Financial Officer (CFO) is considering the call of ` 500 crore of 10-year bonds with a face value of ` 1,000 each, issued five years ago with a callable feature at a coupon rate of 13%. The bonds were issued at a premium of ` 50 per bond, generating total proceeds of ` 525 crore and incurred an initial flotation cost of ` 1.20 crore. They are currently callable at ` 1,100 per bond. To refinance the existing debt, the company plans to issue ` 500 crore of new 11%, 10 year bonds at par value (` 1,000 per bond), with an estimated floatation cost of ` 1.50 crore. The after-tax cost of debt is 8% and floatation cost is written off for tax purpose in five years. The CFO has instructed CA Ankit, Finance Manager, to assess the viability and financial implications of retiring the bonds by the end of March 2026. The company tax rate is 25%.

Avigo Global Limited had entered into a lease arrangement on 1st April 2021 for office space measuring ` 6,000 sq. ft. for a period of 10 years. As per the terms of the agreement, lease payments of ` 6,00,000 per annum were payable in advance at the beginning of each year. The agreement also provided an option to extend the lease for an additional period of 5 years at an annual rent of ` 8,00,000. It is the company's policy to depreciate RoU asset on straight line basis. However, the Company had not shown any intention to exercise the extension option at the time of lease agreement. The Company had incurred initial direct costs amounting to ` 3,00,000 in connection with the lease. The incremental borrowing rate at the commencement date, i.e., 1st April 2021 was 11% per annum. In March 2026, due to space constraints arising from the recruitment of additional staff in the next year, the Company decided to take on lease an additional 3,000 sq. ft. in the same building. It is decided to enter into a separate lease agreement for additional space for a period of 9 years and the additional space will be available for use from March 2027. The incremental borrowing rate as of 31st March 2026 is 12% per annum. The company also decided to exercise the extension option relating to the original 6,000 sq. ft. lease at the end of the year 2025-26.

A team of four officers from Income-tax Department descended upon the company's Head Office at New Delhi on 15th February 2026 to conduct TDS survey under Income-tax Act, 1961. After completing the necessary formalities, they asked for the TDS returns filed during the financial year 2024-25 and three quarters of 2025-26. After perusing the company's accounts, bills and TDS returns, the team summarised following points in the matter of TDS deduction and incidental issues:

(i) Company has made payment of lease rent of ` 24 crore to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Limited located in Gujarat International Finance Tec-City for lease of an aircraft which commenced in June 2025. Neither tax has been deducted on lease rent paid during the year nor these payments have been reported in TDS returns filed by the company.

(ii) Company has paid landing and parking charges of ` 12 crore to Airport Authority of India and 8 crore to private airport operators. The company has deducted tax @ 2% instead of 10% as the payments are in the nature of rent paid for use of space.

(iii) Company has taken an engine on lease in financial year 2025-26 under an arrangement with a foreign company, a tax resident of Germany. Company deducted tax at source @ 10% on lease rental paid as per the provisions contained under DTAA between India and Germany. Chief Accountant informed that foreign company has no permanent establishment in India. However, revenue officers contended that in the absence of furnishing of PAN, TDS is to be deducted at a higher rate of 20% as per the relevant provisions of Income-tax Act, 1961.

(iv) The Company allowed exemptions and deductions to its employees during financial year 2024-25 relevant to Assessment Year 2025-26 on account of House Rent Allowance (HRA), Leave Travel Concession (LTC) and deductions under Chapter VI-A of the Income-tax Act, 1961. In cases, where the monthly rent exceeded ` 10,000, the Company accepted cash rent receipts containing only the address of the property as supporting evidence. Further, exemption in respect of Leave Travel Concession was allowed on self-declaration submitted by employees who had exercised the option to opt out of the new tax regime under Section 115BAC. During verification conducted in financial year 2025-26, it was observed that in 10 cases the rent receipts furnished by employees were not found to be genuine. It was further noticed that in 20 cases, employees ultimately filed their returns of income under the new tax regime and discharged the tax liability accordingly at the time of filing their returns. Before leaving, the income-tax officer issued show cause notice in respect of above issues.

Q5.6.question_text: How can Avigo Global Limited align and integrate all seven elements of the McKinsey's 7S framework to strengthen operational robustness, enhance competitive advantage and prevent a recurrence of operational crisis, while ensuring that each element reinforces the others to create a resilient, efficient and customer-focused airline?
Q5.6.model_answer:
How can Avigo Global Limited align and integrate all seven elements of the McKinsey's 7S framework to strengthen operational robustness, enhance competitive advantage and prevent a recurrence of operational crisis, while ensuring that each element reinforces the others to create a resilient, efficient and customer-focused airline?
(6 Marks)

McKinsey's 7S framework serves as a comprehensive tool for organizations seeking to drive effective change, implement new strategies, and enhance overall performance. It provides a structured way to analyse an organization's internal environment by examining seven interrelated elements: Strategy, Structure, Systems, Shared Values, Skills, Style, and Staff. These elements are not independent; rather, they are interconnected, meaning that a change in one area inevitably influences the others. For instance, revising the organizational strategy may require adjustments in structure, processes, or staff roles to ensure successful execution. 
 
By using the 7S framework, organizations can identify weaknesses and misalignments within their systems, understand how various subsystems influence one another, and develop interventions that are holistic rather than piecemeal. For an organization aiming to achieve its objectives efficiently and sustainably, it is critical that all seven elements are aligned and mutually reinforcing. Proper alignment ensures that the organization's strategy is supported by the right structures, systems, capabilities, culture, leadership style, and talent, thereby maximizing the likelihood of achieving strategic goals and maintaining long-term effectiveness. 
 
Strategy & Structure: Avigo's ultra-low-cost strategy has been instrumental in driving rapid market penetration, high aircraft utilization, and competitive pricing. However, this lean operating model has also exposed operational vulnerabilities, particularly when regulatory changes such as the revised Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL) disrupted tightly optimized schedules. Heavy reliance on minimal buffers, quick turnarounds, and lean crew rosters amplified the impact of such external shocks. To sustain growth, Avigo must embed resilience directly into its core strategy rather than treating it as an afterthought. This includes developing robust contingency planning frameworks that anticipate regulatory, operational, and environmental disruptions. Greater fleet flexibility such as maintaining standby aircraft capacity or diversifying aircraft deployment can reduce cascading delays. Scalable crew management systems, including reserve pools and cross-qualified staff, would further enhance operational continuity. 
 
At the structural level, resilience must be institutionalized through the creation of dedicated risk and crisis management teams with clear authority and accountability. Establishing well-defined escalation pathways ensures rapid decision-making during irregular operations. Stronger cross-functional coordination between operations, crew scheduling, maintenance, and regulatory compliance functions will prevent siloed responses to complex challenges. 
 
By aligning organizational structure with strategic resilience goals, Avigo can protect operational reliability without sacrificing cost discipline. Ultimately, integrating resilience into both strategy and structure enables the airline to balance efficiency with stability, sustaining long-term growth while proactively managing emerging risks. 
 
Systems & Shared Values: The operational disruptions experienced during the November 2025 crisis underscored the critical importance of robust, modernized systems in airline operations. Large-scale instability revealed how vulnerabilities in crew rostering. flight scheduling, and real-time coordination can rapidly escalate across an entire network. For Avigo, this serves as a defining lesson that advanced operational systems are not merely efficiency tools but strategic enablers of resilience. Modernized crew rostering and dynamic flight scheduling platforms must be capable of responding instantly to irregular operations. Automated real-time monitoring systems should detect cascading delays, crew legality risks, aircraft rotation conflicts, and airport constraints as they emerge. Such visibility enables operations control teams to implement corrective measures before disruptions spread further. Predictive analytics strengthens this framework by forecasting demand surges, adverse weather risks, and crew fatigue exposure under Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL). Proactive resource reallocation within regulatory constraints helps prevent network-wide strain. 
 
However, technology alone cannot safeguard operational stability. Systems must be reinforced by shared organizational values that prioritize safety, reliability, transparency, and customer trust alongside cost efficiency. Embedding disciplined decision-making ensures regulatory obligations are upheld and communication remains responsible. Employees across operations control, crew planning, ground handling, and customer service must consistently adhere to established protocols. A culture grounded in accountability, proactive communication, and continuous improvement transforms technology into a force multiplier. By integrating advanced systems with deeply embedded values, AviGo can build structural resilience and maintain passenger confidence even under severe crisis conditions. 
 
Style and Staff: Leadership style plays a key role in cascading strategic priorities and operational discipline throughout the organization. A participative, transparent, and proactive leadership style encourages reporting of early warning signs, fosters collaboration across departments, and drives adherence to safety and operational standards. This, in turn, empowers staff especially pilots, crew, and operational planners, to take ownership of scheduling, contingency measures, and passenger service. By investing in workforce planning, training, and retention, Avigo ensures adequate staffing depth, flexible scheduling, and a motivated team capable of maintaining operational continuity even under stress. 
 
Skills: The skills element ties all others together. Even with the right strategy, structure, systems, values, style, and staffing, a lack of skills in predictive analytics, network planning, crisis management, and regulatory compliance can undermine operations. Developing these competencies ensures that the workforce can fully leverage systems, execute the strategy, uphold shared values, and support the leadership style. Skilled teams improve operational efficiency, reduce errors, and enhance passenger satisfaction, solidifying Avigo's competitive advantage and reducing vulnerability to future crises. 
 
Overall, by interlinking all seven S's, Avigo can transform its lean operational model into one that is both efficient and resilient. Strategy guides priorities, structure supports execution, systems provide real-time control, shared values drive responsible behaviour, leadership...

Avigo Global Limited can use the McKinsey 7S Framework to ensure that all parts of the organisation work together in a coordinated manner. The operational crisis faced by the company showed that although Avigo was successful in controlling costs, there was lack of proper coordination between people, systems and operational planning. Therefore, all seven elements of the McKinsey 7S Model should be aligned to build a strong, efficient and customer-focused airline.

Strategy: Avigo should redesign its strategy by balancing low-cost operations with operational stability and customer satisfaction. Earlier, the company focused heavily on aggressive cost control and lean staffing, which created problems when new FDTL rules reduced pilot availability. The company should now focus not only on cost reduction but also on investing in technology, workforce planning and operational resilience. The decision to invest 3% of annual revenue in IT infrastructure and AI-based rostering systems is an important strategic step. The strategy should also focus on punctuality, safety, service quality and customer trust.

Structure: The organisational structure of Avigo should support quick decision-making and better coordination between departments. During the crisis, poor coordination between crew management, operations and scheduling worsened the situation. Formation of the Crisis Management Group (CMG) was an important step. The company should further create dedicated teams for operational planning, disruption management and customer support. Better coordination among departments would help the airline respond quickly during emergencies and reduce operational disruptions.

Systems: Avigo's systems for crew rostering and disruption management were outdated and insufficient. The company should introduce AI-based crew scheduling systems, real-time monitoring systems and automated disruption management tools. These systems would help in proper allocation of pilots, compliance with FDTL rules and better handling of cancellations and delays. Strong systems would improve efficiency, reduce manual errors and help the company identify problems at an early stage.

Shared Values: The company should build a culture where safety, customer satisfaction, operational reliability and employee well-being are given equal importance along with profitability. Earlier, the organisation focused excessively on cost cutting, which affected operational stability. Management should encourage a culture of accountability, teamwork and proactive problem-solving. Shared values focused on customer service and safety would improve trust among passengers and employees.

Skills: The crisis highlighted gaps in workforce planning, technology management and crisis handling skills. Avigo should provide regular training to pilots, operational staff and management teams on AI systems, disruption handling and customer service. Recruitment of additional pilots and administrative staff would also improve operational capability. Developing employee skills would help the company handle future challenges more effectively.

Staff: Avigo's lean staffing model became inadequate after the new FDTL rules reduced pilot flying hours. The company should maintain adequate staff strength and reserve crew capacity to handle operational disruptions. Recruitment should focus not only on pilots but also on operational and customer service staff. Better work schedules and employee welfare measures would improve morale and reduce fatigue. Proper workforce planning would strengthen operational continuity.

Style: The leadership style of the company should become more proactive, participative and transparent. Earlier, management appeared too focused on cost optimisation and delayed responding to warning signals related to pilot shortages. The leadership should now encourage open communication, teamwork and timely reporting of operational issues. Active involvement of Independent Directors in the Crisis Management Group reflects a stronger governance-oriented approach. A supportive leadership style would improve employee confidence and organisational discipline.

Integrated Alignment of 7S Elements

All seven elements of the McKinsey 7S Framework are interconnected and should support each other. Avigo's new strategy of balancing cost efficiency with operational resilience should be supported by strong systems, adequate staffing, skilled employees and effective leadership. Shared values should encourage safety, customer focus and operational discipline across the organisation.

By properly aligning all seven elements, Avigo can build a more resilient, efficient and customer-focused airline. This would help the company prevent future operational crises, strengthen customer trust and maintain its leadership position in the Indian aviation industry.

The provisions of DTAAs, along with sections 4, 5, 9, 90 & 91 of the Act, are relevant while applying the provisions of tax deduction at source. Therefore, section 206AA of the Act cannot be understood to override charging sections 4 and 5 of the Act.

Accordingly, the Apex Court held that where the tax has been deducted on the strength of the beneficial provisions of DTAAs, the provisions of section 206AA cannot be invoked to insist on the tax deduction @ 20%, having regard to the overriding nature of the provisions of section 90(2).

(iii) Short deduction of tax at source on landing and parking charges

With respect to short deduction of tax at source on landing and parking charges, the company had relied on Supreme Court ruling in case of Japan Airlines Co. Ltd. v. CIT / CIT v. Singapore Airlines Ltd. (2015) 377 ITR 372, wherein the Apex Court held that the landing and parking charges which are fixed by the Airports Authority of India and private airport operators are not merely for the "use of the land". These charges are also for services and facilities offered in connection with the aircraft operation at the airport, which include providing of air traffic services, ground safety services, aeronautical communication facilities, installation and maintenance of navigational aids and meteorological services at the airport [(SC)]. Thus, tax is not deductible under section 194-I, which provides deduction of tax for payment in the nature of rent.

Accordingly, the company had deducted tax at source @2% under section 194C on payment of ` 12 crores and ` 8 crores made towards landing and parking charges to the Airports Authority of India and private airport operators, respectively.

     Hard S and soft S.

     The hard S comprises of
     Strategy, Structure and System.
     It is easy for managers
     to influence and change these

     and defined and deal with facts
     and rules.

     While soft areas are 4S

     to describe.

feedback.what_went_right: You correctly identified that the McKinsey 7S Framework has seven elements and made a basic distinction between 'Hard S' (Strategy, Structure, Systems) and 'Soft S' (Style, Staff, Skills, Shared Values). That's the only reason you got 0.5 marks — you at least named the framework and its components.
feedback.what_went_wrong: Almost everything that the question actually asked for is missing from your answer. The question was specifically about Avigo Global Limited and how all seven elements should be aligned and integrated to prevent an operational crisis. You wrote a generic textbook definition of the 7S framework instead of applying it to the case. You never discussed Strategy in the context of Avigo's ultra-low-cost model and FDTL disruptions. You never talked about how Structure should support crisis management and cross-functional coordination. You skipped Systems entirely — no mention of AI-based rostering, real-time monitoring, or predictive analytics. Shared Values, Skills, Staff, and Style were not discussed at all in any applied sense. Most critically, you completely ignored the integration and alignment aspect — how each 'S' reinforces the others — which was the core of the question. The examiner was looking for a holistic, case-specific analysis, not a definition of what Hard S and Soft S mean.
feedback.conclusion: This answer shows you know the framework exists, but you haven't practiced applying it to real scenarios — which is exactly what CA exams test. Start practicing case-based application of every framework you study, and you'll see a dramatic improvement in your scores.
