McKinsey's 7S framework serves as a comprehensive tool for organizations seeking to drive effective change, implement new strategies, and enhance overall performance. It provides a structured way to analyse an organization's internal environment by examining seven interrelated elements: Strategy, Structure, Systems, Shared Values, Skills, Style, and Staff. These elements are not independent; rather, they are interconnected, meaning that a change in one area inevitably influences the others. For instance, revising the organizational strategy may require adjustments in structure, processes, or staff roles to ensure successful execution. 
 
By using the 7S framework, organizations can identify weaknesses and misalignments within their systems, understand how various subsystems influence one another, and develop interventions that are holistic rather than piecemeal. For an organization aiming to achieve its objectives efficiently and sustainably, it is critical that all seven elements are aligned and mutually reinforcing. Proper alignment ensures that the organization's strategy is supported by the right structures, systems, capabilities, culture, leadership style, and talent, thereby maximizing the likelihood of achieving strategic goals and maintaining long-term effectiveness. 
 
Strategy & Structure: Avigo's ultra-low-cost strategy has been instrumental in driving rapid market penetration, high aircraft utilization, and competitive pricing. However, this lean operating model has also exposed operational vulnerabilities, particularly when regulatory changes such as the revised Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL) disrupted tightly optimized schedules. Heavy reliance on minimal buffers, quick turnarounds, and lean crew rosters amplified the impact of such external shocks. To sustain growth, Avigo must embed resilience directly into its core strategy rather than treating it as an afterthought. This includes developing robust contingency planning frameworks that anticipate regulatory, operational, and environmental disruptions. Greater fleet flexibility such as maintaining standby aircraft capacity or diversifying aircraft deployment can reduce cascading delays. Scalable crew management systems, including reserve pools and cross-qualified staff, would further enhance operational continuity. 
 
At the structural level, resilience must be institutionalized through the creation of dedicated risk and crisis management teams with clear authority and accountability. Establishing well-defined escalation pathways ensures rapid decision-making during irregular operations. Stronger cross-functional coordination between operations, crew scheduling, maintenance, and regulatory compliance functions will prevent siloed responses to complex challenges. 
 
By aligning organizational structure with strategic resilience goals, Avigo can protect operational reliability without sacrificing cost discipline. Ultimately, integrating resilience into both strategy and structure enables the airline to balance efficiency with stability, sustaining long-term growth while proactively managing emerging risks. 
 
Systems & Shared Values: The operational disruptions experienced during the November 2025 crisis underscored the critical importance of robust, modernized systems in airline operations. Large-scale instability revealed how vulnerabilities in crew rostering. flight scheduling, and real-time coordination can rapidly escalate across an entire network. For Avigo, this serves as a defining lesson that advanced operational systems are not merely efficiency tools but strategic enablers of resilience. Modernized crew rostering and dynamic flight scheduling platforms must be capable of responding instantly to irregular operations. Automated real-time monitoring systems should detect cascading delays, crew legality risks, aircraft rotation conflicts, and airport constraints as they emerge. Such visibility enables operations control teams to implement corrective measures before disruptions spread further. Predictive analytics strengthens this framework by forecasting demand surges, adverse weather risks, and crew fatigue exposure under Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL). Proactive resource reallocation within regulatory constraints helps prevent network-wide strain. 
 
However, technology alone cannot safeguard operational stability. Systems must be reinforced by shared organizational values that prioritize safety, reliability, transparency, and customer trust alongside cost efficiency. Embedding disciplined decision-making ensures regulatory obligations are upheld and communication remains responsible. Employees across operations control, crew planning, ground handling, and customer service must consistently adhere to established protocols. A culture grounded in accountability, proactive communication, and continuous improvement transforms technology into a force multiplier. By integrating advanced systems with deeply embedded values, AviGo can build structural resilience and maintain passenger confidence even under severe crisis conditions. 
 
Style and Staff: Leadership style plays a key role in cascading strategic priorities and operational discipline throughout the organization. A participative, transparent, and proactive leadership style encourages reporting of early warning signs, fosters collaboration across departments, and drives adherence to safety and operational standards. This, in turn, empowers staff especially pilots, crew, and operational planners, to take ownership of scheduling, contingency measures, and passenger service. By investing in workforce planning, training, and retention, Avigo ensures adequate staffing depth, flexible scheduling, and a motivated team capable of maintaining operational continuity even under stress. 
 
Skills: The skills element ties all others together. Even with the right strategy, structure, systems, values, style, and staffing, a lack of skills in predictive analytics, network planning, crisis management, and regulatory compliance can undermine operations. Developing these competencies ensures that the workforce can fully leverage systems, execute the strategy, uphold shared values, and support the leadership style. Skilled teams improve operational efficiency, reduce errors, and enhance passenger satisfaction, solidifying Avigo's competitive advantage and reducing vulnerability to future crises. 
 
Overall, by interlinking all seven S's, Avigo can transform its lean operational model into one that is both efficient and resilient. Strategy guides priorities, structure supports execution, systems provide real-time control, shared values drive responsible behaviour, leadership...

Avigo Global Limited can use the McKinsey 7S Framework to ensure that all parts of the organisation work together in a coordinated manner. The operational crisis faced by the company showed that although Avigo was successful in controlling costs, there was lack of proper coordination between people, systems and operational planning. Therefore, all seven elements of the McKinsey 7S Model should be aligned to build a strong, efficient and customer-focused airline.

Strategy: Avigo should redesign its strategy by balancing low-cost operations with operational stability and customer satisfaction. Earlier, the company focused heavily on aggressive cost control and lean staffing, which created problems when new FDTL rules reduced pilot availability. The company should now focus not only on cost reduction but also on investing in technology, workforce planning and operational resilience. The decision to invest 3% of annual revenue in IT infrastructure and AI-based rostering systems is an important strategic step. The strategy should also focus on punctuality, safety, service quality and customer trust.

Structure: The organisational structure of Avigo should support quick decision-making and better coordination between departments. During the crisis, poor coordination between crew management, operations and scheduling worsened the situation. Formation of the Crisis Management Group (CMG) was an important step. The company should further create dedicated teams for operational planning, disruption management and customer support. Better coordination among departments would help the airline respond quickly during emergencies and reduce operational disruptions.

Systems: Avigo's systems for crew rostering and disruption management were outdated and insufficient. The company should introduce AI-based crew scheduling systems, real-time monitoring systems and automated disruption management tools. These systems would help in proper allocation of pilots, compliance with FDTL rules and better handling of cancellations and delays. Strong systems would improve efficiency, reduce manual errors and help the company identify problems at an early stage.

Shared Values: The company should build a culture where safety, customer satisfaction, operational reliability and employee well-being are given equal importance along with profitability. Earlier, the organisation focused excessively on cost cutting, which affected operational stability. Management should encourage a culture of accountability, teamwork and proactive problem-solving. Shared values focused on customer service and safety would improve trust among passengers and employees.

Skills: The crisis highlighted gaps in workforce planning, technology management and crisis handling skills. Avigo should provide regular training to pilots, operational staff and management teams on AI systems, disruption handling and customer service. Recruitment of additional pilots and administrative staff would also improve operational capability. Developing employee skills would help the company handle future challenges more effectively.

Staff: Avigo's lean staffing model became inadequate after the new FDTL rules reduced pilot flying hours. The company should maintain adequate staff strength and reserve crew capacity to handle operational disruptions. Recruitment should focus not only on pilots but also on operational and customer service staff. Better work schedules and employee welfare measures would improve morale and reduce fatigue. Proper workforce planning would strengthen operational continuity.

Style: The leadership style of the company should become more proactive, participative and transparent. Earlier, management appeared too focused on cost optimisation and delayed responding to warning signals related to pilot shortages. The leadership should now encourage open communication, teamwork and timely reporting of operational issues. Active involvement of Independent Directors in the Crisis Management Group reflects a stronger governance-oriented approach. A supportive leadership style would improve employee confidence and organisational discipline.

Integrated Alignment of 7S Elements

All seven elements of the McKinsey 7S Framework are interconnected and should support each other. Avigo's new strategy of balancing cost efficiency with operational resilience should be supported by strong systems, adequate staffing, skilled employees and effective leadership. Shared values should encourage safety, customer focus and operational discipline across the organisation.

By properly aligning all seven elements, Avigo can build a more resilient, efficient and customer-focused airline. This would help the company prevent future operational crises, strengthen customer trust and maintain its leadership position in the Indian aviation industry.

5.8 (i) Non deduction of tax at source on lease rent paid to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Ltd.  
 
No tax was deductible on lease rent paid to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Limited, being a unit located in IFSC, GIFT City, engaged in aircraft leasing business and eligible for deduction under section 80LA in view of the CBDT Notification No. 65/2022 dated 16.06.2022 issued under section 197A(1F). The said Notification specifically provides that no tax is required to be deducted under section 194-I on such lease rentals subject to prescribed conditions. Since the prescribed declaration/ documents were obtained from the PQR Fleet Services IFSC Ltd, the Company bona fide did not deduct tax at source. 
 
However, since these transactions have not been reported in the TDS returns filed during the P.Y. 2025-26, the company should file revised TDS returns and report the transactions specifying the reasons for non-deduction. 
(ii) Short deduction of tax at source on lease rent of engine paid to foreign company 
 
The Company deducted tax at source @10% on engine lease rental paid to German company in accordance with the India-Germany DTAA. The non-resident payee does not have any Permanent Establishment in India and the applicable DTAA rate being more beneficial, the same is applicable by virtue of section 90(2).  
 
Further, with respect to deducting tax at source at the rate specified in DTAA though tax has to be deducted at higher rates in the absence of furnishing of PAN, the company had relied on the Supreme Court ruling in case of CIT (International Taxation) v. Air India Ltd. [2023] 456 ITR 139 wherein the Apex Court noted that section 90(2) provides that the provisions of the DTAAs would override the provisions of the Act in cases where the provisions of DTAAs are more beneficial to the assessee. Even the charging sections 4 and 5 of the Act, which deal with the principle of ascertainment of total income under the Act, are also subordinate to the principle enshrined in section 90(2). 
 
The provisions of tax withholding, i.e., section 195 of the Act, would apply only to sums that are otherwise chargeable to tax under the Act.

The provisions of DTAAs, along with sections 4, 5, 9, 90 & 91 of the Act, are relevant while applying the provisions of tax deduction at source. Therefore, section 206AA of the Act cannot be understood to override charging sections 4 and 5 of the Act.

Accordingly, the Apex Court held that where the tax has been deducted on the strength of the beneficial provisions of DTAAs, the provisions of section 206AA cannot be invoked to insist on the tax deduction @ 20%, having regard to the overriding nature of the provisions of section 90(2).

(iii) Short deduction of tax at source on landing and parking charges

With respect to short deduction of tax at source on landing and parking charges, the company had relied on Supreme Court ruling in case of Japan Airlines Co. Ltd. v. CIT / CIT v. Singapore Airlines Ltd. (2015) 377 ITR 372, wherein the Apex Court held that the landing and parking charges which are fixed by the Airports Authority of India and private airport operators are not merely for the "use of the land". These charges are also for services and facilities offered in connection with the aircraft operation at the airport, which include providing of air traffic services, ground safety services, aeronautical communication facilities, installation and maintenance of navigational aids and meteorological services at the airport [(SC)]. Thus, tax is not deductible under section 194-I, which provides deduction of tax for payment in the nature of rent.

Accordingly, the company had deducted tax at source @2% under section 194C on payment of ` 12 crores and ` 8 crores made towards landing and parking charges to the Airports Authority of India and private airport operators, respectively.
